text_clean: Vale la pena pagar la entrada. El circuito es muy ordenado, siempre con guía, español o ingles, solo hay que esperar. Tiene un pequeño museo, tiene un huerto con algunas plantas típicas del Perú. También hay tiendas de recuerdos, pero también los puedes encontrar en los mercados incas. La guía muy amable y sabía bastante, hasta la llama se le acerco a preguntar. El recorrido es cerca de una hora. Recomendado si visitas Lima. También cuenta con un restaurante, pero yo solo visite la huaca. ^^"

text_clean: I understand how Machu Picchu could once have been named one of the seven wonders of the modern world upon its discovery. A sacred place, suspended in the mountains and untouched by time — it must have felt like magic. But that time is long gone. Today, it’s overwhelmed by mass tourism. What truly broke it for me was seeing tourists cross safety barriers just to laugh and take selfies, ignoring the guards like misbehaving children. It made me question why I came at all. A place like this should be empty, inaccessible, preserved. I won’t return. I honestly hope it’s one day closed to protect its beauty and the peace it deserves. But in a capitalist world with millions of people all following the same paths — myself included — that probably won’t happen any time soon

text_clean: Desde el ingreso al parque es necesario caminar con cautela, puesto que cada 5 min es posible que un trenecito con niños abordo se cruce en tu camino. El parque perdió sentido desde que incluyeron juegos mecánicos, es irónico como es que no esta permitido el ingreso a las áreas verdes pero estas vienen siendo ocupadas por puestos de venta de alimentos y bebidas y muchos otros por juegos que encarecen la vista del parque y dañan las áreas verdes, es un tipo de contaminación visual. Otro punto a resaltar es la limpieza de los servicios higiénicos, se entiende que si se realizara un cobro por el acceso debe invertirse en su mejora y limpieza. Por último pero no menos importante, si van a cobrar una entrada que sea para el acceso de ambos parques, los espectáculos pierden sentido por volverse comercial. 0/5

text_clean: This is my third time here and let me tell you there’s been tons of changes. After you get to Aguas Calientes, everyone whether you hired a guide or not must now go to the Ministerio de Culturas and stand in line just to get a number to return later that night or the next day. My understanding from talking to other tour guides is that tour guides used to be able to buy your tickets and they would buy at the Peruvian National price and resell at the international tourist price. To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

Pero con ponerlo en su sitio basta. Es lamentable que tengan personal no apto para atender a los turistas y público en general. . . De igual forma. Mi recomendación es que vayan a visitar el lugar. Les encantará.

label: neutro

text_clean: It’s not a criticism of the place but the people that run it. As usual in Peru the info you can see on Google hasn’t been updated in a long time. The minimum effort on their part would be to actually update the info, a thing that I’ve done now three times instead of the actual f. people who work in those touristic places cause I thought that would avoid other people getting the same disappointment I had. Also I did ask the guy who was selling the tickets and announced me ‘oh no this timetable is from before the pandemic’ : Me > ‘so I’m curious, why haven’t you updated the timetable info then?’ Him > just shrugging shoulders. Yeah.

text_clean: No existe comunicación entre el teleferico y Kuelap, a pesar de ser la única opción para llegar (sino debes caminar más de 4 hrs). No compren las entradas por adelantado, si te pasas de la hora no te dejan entrar y si hay algún problema no te cambian la entrada. En el teleferico hay un pequeño stand y ahi puedes comprarlas (solo por internet!) Es un bello lugar pero la administración deja mucho que desear, solo puedes hacer 1 recorrido, así que hazlo lento porque no te permiten retroceder en el camino. Me voy con la sensación de que solo les interesa el dinero y no se preocupan del turista, ni de la experiencia vivida.

text_clean: Pretty nice and interesting historical place, if a little overhyped. Our experience was dampened by a terrible guide who repeated the same 3-4 facts that could be found in any guidebook over the course of 45mins. Afterwards we went round again and there was enough information available on the walls and the leaflet you get given with entry (unlike in several other attractions in Peru) to enjoy a slower self guided tour. That being said, we heard a couple of more diligent guides while on our second lap so perhaps we were just unlucky.

text_clean: The place itself is certainly unique and very special. The organization and the associated bureaucracy is a disgrace, also because 1.7 million people visit this site every year, the organization should be improved. At first, our train didn't run and we had to wait in a larger group with two small children in undignified conditions in front of a gate with hundreds of others. We spent a total of over 1600 dollars on the train tickets alone, so you would at least expect decent conditions. After a delay of around 2.5 hours, we boarded the train. The information policy at the station was practically non-existent. On the day of the tour (after all the necessary documents had been submitted), our tickets were checked a total of 5 times (!!!) and we were asked for all our IDs 4 times (in a group of 10 people). At the entrance to the Puchho Piccho we had to wait again for about 15 minutes (in the rain), there was again bureaucratic dung, if necessary it would have been helpful if the person had not been alone. If you now think about who is enriching themselves in this place and on the way there, the whole thing is outrageous for the Peruvian people. A few rich people are getting much richer here and the poor remain poor. But it is inconceivable that the organization and processes associated with the visit are not being improved.

text_clean: So disappointed to have made a 40 minute journey across town to see Huaca Pucilana after reading about the site in an official leaflet, only to be told that you need a prior appointment that wasn't advertised on the leaflet I had. Upon showing the leaflet to the attendant, I'd have thought they would have showed a little more consideration given the leaflet was one of their own. Unfortunately there are times when common sense is outweighed by someone wearing a uniform, and they'd much prefer to sit around twiddling their thumbs. A walk around the site enabled us to get an idea of the sheer scale of the place, and to witness a guide showing only 2 people around. Really busy. To see their llamas caged in to such a small area when there's so much space for them to roam is a complete mystery to me. Disgraceful mistreatment of animals.

text_clean: Me encanto la vibra y la paz que se siente en este lugar, es increíble. Hace honor a su nombramiento como Maravilla del mundo. Yo fui con una agencia lo cual me facilitó todo, me esperaba un guía en cada momento para acompañar todo mi recorrido. Muchas personas deciden hacerlo por su cuenta sin embargo por los tiempos creo que con guía puede ser mejor y más ordenado. Para subir yo subí en transporte el cual tardo 20 min pero se hace fila de una hora previa a subir al transporte. Hay personas que lo hacen caminando y al parecer es aproximadamente dos horas para subir pero vale totalmente la pena cuando ya estás arriba.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Vista la importancia del hallazgo, Walter Alva impulsó la construcción del Museo Tumbas Reales de Sipan, que fue inaugurado en el 2002. Está ubicado en Lambayeque, y su edificación se inspiró en las antiguas pirámides truncadas de la prehispánica Civilización Moche (siglo I a VII d. C.). El museo custodia más de dos mil piezas de oro. Sin lugar a dudas, el principal atractivo es la tumba del Señor de Sipán, con sus acompañantes y sus respectivos ajuares funerarios. La estructura, en un área techada de 3.156,45 m², tiene tres pisos. El acceso es a través de una rampa de 74.21 metros de largo, tal y como se accedía a los antiguos templos moches. Dicha pendiente conduce al tercer piso. La visita se realiza de arriba hacia abajo reviviendo la experiencia del descubridor del Señor de Sipan. Desde el segundo piso, se observa la réplica exacta de la cámara funeraria del antiguo dignatario moche llamado el Señor de Sipan y saber cómo fue su cultura, sus logros tecnológicos y su pensamiento sobre la muerte, entre otros aspectos. El centro del museo contiene la Sala Real Mochica donde se encuentra el Señor de Sipan con su ropa de guerrero y pectoral de oro y otras joyas del mismo material; la cámara funeraria del antiguo gobernante, junto a ocho esqueletos de sus acompañantes; otros dignatarios descubiertos, como el sacerdote y el Viejo Señor de Sipán, de la misma investidura que el gobernador moche, todos con sus respectivas vestimentas originales. En esta Sala Real Mochica, hay 35 maniquíes que representan su entorno político, diez de los cuales han sido articulados, por lo que están en movimiento durante las visitas. Esta reconstrucción exacta de lo que fue toda una magnificiencia de la corte real del guerrero moche representa fielmente lo que fue un séquito Mochica. También se pueden ver los ornamentos recuperados del sepulcro, como es el caso del protector coxal de oro, entre otros importantes objetos. Se incluye un mensaje de lo que dejó el saqueo y la importancia de la protección de la herencia cultural del Perú. Hay parqueo de autos, con seguridad, ambiente amplio. Se recomienda visitar el museo para conocer y aprender más de nuestra historia, costumbre y procedencia de nuestros pasados.

La estructura, en un área techada de 3.156,45 m², tiene tres pisos. El acceso es a través de una rampa de 74.21 metros de largo, tal y como se accedía a los antiguos templos moches. Dicha pendiente conduce al tercer piso. La visita se realiza de arriba hacia abajo reviviendo la experiencia del descubridor del Señor de Sipan. Desde el segundo piso, se observa la réplica exacta de la cámara funeraria del antiguo dignatario moche llamado el Señor de Sipan y saber cómo fue su cultura, sus logros tecnológicos y su pensamiento sobre la muerte, entre otros aspectos. El centro del museo contiene la Sala Real Mochica donde se encuentra el Señor de Sipan con su ropa de guerrero y pectoral de oro y otras joyas del mismo material; la cámara funeraria del antiguo gobernante, junto a ocho esqueletos de sus acompañantes; otros dignatarios descubiertos, como el sacerdote y el Viejo Señor de Sipán, de la misma investidura que el gobernador moche, todos con sus respectivas vestimentas originales. En esta Sala Real Mochica, hay 35 maniquíes que representan su entorno político, diez de los cuales han sido articulados, por lo que están en movimiento durante las visitas. Esta reconstrucción exacta de lo que fue toda una magnificiencia de la corte real del guerrero moche representa fielmente lo que fue un séquito Mochica. También se pueden ver los ornamentos recuperados del sepulcro, como es el caso del protector coxal de oro, entre otros importantes objetos. Se incluye un mensaje de lo que dejó el saqueo y la importancia de la protección de la herencia cultural del Perú. Hay parqueo de autos, con seguridad, ambiente amplio. Se recomienda visitar el museo para conocer y aprender más de nuestra historia, costumbre y procedencia de nuestros pasados.

text_clean: Fascinating study of the dedication of the ancients in building structures covering great areas with handmade vertical bricks. On some you can even see their hand and finger prints! Our guide shared a wide scope of knowledge from all of those who lived here, their customs, foods they planted and consumed, burial rites, beliefs, skills... He was a great guide to his heritage and this enormous site in the middle of the city. (Unfortunately, there is a listing for a "minizoo" here. While seeing the 7 famous llamas, alpacas and dozens+ guinea pigs was an interesting part of the tour...that does Not qualify the Very minimum pen and feeding bin as a "zoo." There is a genuine one elsewhere. The handful of indigenousanimals here are just to enlighten visitors as to the animals the original denizens had for food, like the well-planted garden.) This is a healthy, enlightening educational walk through an amazing ancient world. (PS- Please bring some currency to show your appreciation to your fascinating guide!!)

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Together with the Rainbow Mountains and the Machu Picchu my highlight of visiting Peru. The lines look absolutely stunning from the airplane. Got to take so many great pictures of the different figures. Take a motion sickness pill before the flight as the winds are quite strong and the planes are very small. Most providers do hand out some strong smelling alcohol on a cotton pad to put in front of your nose to get you through the whole of the flight in case you do get motion sick. Once on the ground, the facility also has a room for you to lie down and recover. Great place to buy some Nazca unique souvenirs you won't find anywhere else in Peru.

text_clean: 1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

text_clean: As far as most historic centers go in Latin American metropolisis, this is definitely one that has been taken care and preserved by the government and it's citizenry. It's historical buildings and facades showcase the architecture that was prevalent during the colonial era and it's promenades, plazas, government palaces and Catholic places of worship are grand buildings worthy of preservation and conservation efforts for generations to come. It's beautiful but like all great modern cosmopolitan cities, Limas historic center also has pickpockets and people of ill-will looking out for bedazzled tourists sporting their wealth through their jewelry, flimsy backpacks, and expensive camera equipment. This place is worth the visit but just be extremely aware of your surroundings at all times and wary of "friendly" strangers who approach you attempting to make casual conversation. destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: This is a cool historical site surrounded by sea of police corruption. We were there in December and five (5) police shakedowns later, my son and I made it from the Nazca desert back to Lima. Our strategies included dividing up the $100 dollars the Hilton gave us on credit (after the police stole all of our money a day earlier). We divided the $ and hid it in various locations in the car. Each time I would reload my wallet with only $20 dollars. Each time the Peru Police pulled us over and demanded money for not having a fire extinguisher in the car, for having the lights on, for NOT having the lights on...they just pulled out their little book of violations and pointed to a violation and demanded payment on the spot. Each time my wallet only had $20 and we said that was all we had and we would be happy to pay by credit card (which of course they could not take...). We kept our original passports hidden and just used the copies of our documents that the Hilton made for us. We would say that "the police already kept our original documents and we don't have them anymore and are going to the American Consulate in Lima to ask for them to be replaced." One group of police actually let us go with no payment at all...so that was positive.... I've been to 84 countries. This is the most corrupt place that I have ever seen. Sometimes the traffic fines come with death threats. Seeing the lines by aircraft was interesting but not worth the price of all the police terrorism to get down there from Lima.

One group of police actually let us go with no payment at all...so that was positive.... I've been to 84 countries. This is the most corrupt place that I have ever seen. Sometimes the traffic fines come with death threats. Seeing the lines by aircraft was interesting but not worth the price of all the police terrorism to get down there from Lima.
label: neutro

text_clean: Manchu Picchu is a beautiful destination and a place that everyone should see one in their lives. Got lucky. Weather was perfect … in March. The train ride from Cusco was also pretty. Taking the bus up the steep slope along a narrow road of switchbacks and no guardrail was definitely an experience I won’t forget.

text_clean: El cañon del colca tiene un paisaje imponente e inmenso, mucha flora y fauna. Hay que ir con buen calzado y un nivel intermedio de estado físico ya que hay que hacer trekking en altura y resulta medio dificultoso si no estás acostumbrado. Es viaje es bastante extenso, más o menos son 2 o 3 horas para llegar. No tuve la suerte de ver ningún Cóndor pero fue una linda experiencia. Esta excursión no la recomiendo hacer si vas a Perú con los días contados, hay lugares mejores.

text_clean: We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

text_clean: Si puedes ve con un guía, ellos te explican la historia del monasterio de una manera muy dinámica (la guía Claudia es muy buena relatando). Es un lugar muy pintoresco por los colores de las calles, la arquitectura es curiosa, el arte hermoso y la vista desde la cúpula es genial. Dato para los estudiantes, lleven su carnet universitario para pagar la entrada de 12 soles. Adentro venden artesanías desde joyería hasta una crema de pétalos de rosa preparada por las monjas. Además cuentan con una cafetería con precios un poco alto.

text_clean: Stunning place - but for the life of us we could not work out what the 70 soles per person fee was going to. The whole thing stank of corruption and lining local officials pockets - we were told it was for tourism infrastructure, but this was few and far between. And as a tourist I'm already paying for my hotel, transport and food that is going to benefit the local economy, I'm not completely sure what extra this was going to - just a payment to look at nature? Yes, the roads were good, but I'm pretty sure that's more because of the plethora of mining companies in the area. Seemed just like seeing foreign tourists as open wallets and was definitely not a great side to see of Peru.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nazca Lines is a great art work on the very large Canvas of Earth, carved out by excavating the soil of the Nazca Desert in southern Peru.They were created between 500 BC and 500 AD. No modern technology no modern Canvas at that time. These large geoglyphs are indeed wonderful world of art . Mirror to advancement of human creativity. Most lines run straight across the landscape. There are also depiction of animals and plants, made up of lines. The combined length of all the lines is over 1,300 km. It is preserved till today naturally due to its isolation and the dry, windless, stable climate of the plateau. But anthropogenic interference may obliterate it's beauty in coming years. In 1994 this has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Great salute to the artists selecting the Nature's Canvas which stood the test of time for the last 2500 years. Good luck.

text_clean: I paid 100 PEN with an agency to get here which included transportation, meal, and entry. I think you need to buy entry tickets in advance. There were a couple small areas on each end that were closed, but you can still see everything. Pictures are less picturesque because there is a tin roof over the entryway walls. I consider it “open” and wouldn’t be deterred by the reviews saying it’s closed or partially closed. I think the biggest difference between Machu Pichu and here is that Machu Pichu is in a saddle so you can climb a mountain for those famous pictures. Kuelap is the top of the mountain so you can’t photograph the whole city. My tour was in Spanish, so I didn’t understand everything, but the views and experience were good. The walls are meant to hold in the dirt. The Chachapoya essentially created some level space to build on and it’s not considered a fort it’s a religious site. Lastly, the gondola (cabinas) station is marked permanently closed, but it’s actually open and located where it’s supposed to be on the map. You board a bus there and they take you further up the mountain to get onto the gondola.

text_clean: It is an amazing experience to reach el Cañon del Colca in Arequipa. It is the world's third deepest canyon in the world. You will have the opportunity to see the nearby villages and the many activities they provide. Once you reach el mirador la Cruz del Cóndor, you will be delighted to see the condors as they fly across the sky and dive deep into the canyon. Sunscreen, hat and water are a must during this helada season. Walk at your own pace to enjoy the beautiful views and take lots of pictures, cheers! ✨️

text_clean: Hermoso museo de sitio arqueológico. Si uno está o visita el distrito de Miraflores es una picardía no visitar Huaca Pucllana. Los guías son muy buenos, el paseo tiene una duración de alrededor de hora y media o dos. Es recomendable llevar sombrero por el sol y buen calzado. La arena y el reflejo del lugar pueden jugar una mala pasada si no se lleva ropa apropiada. De todas formas, si uno se cansa, puede pedir al guía y éste le indica por dónde abandonar el recorrido. Hay un restaurante con platos típicos muy bueno dentro del predio. Si se busca algo más económico alrededor hay buenas opciones.

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Falta mejorar los servicios, el cobro por el ingreso a las islas ballestas puede ser, pero para el ingreso a las playas no, ya que las playas tenemos derecho al ingreso todos los peruanos, se ve como si las hallán pivatizado, el pago del derecho por parqueo al lugar donde se embarcan para ir a las islas ballestas me parece un exeso, 5 soles la hora, osea cada usuario esta obligado a pagar 15 soles, costa de cochera mas caro que en Lima, las pistas para las playas estan bien deterioradas, llenas de huecos, encalaminados y trochas por tramos y tampoco se ve personal que este dando mantenimiento a las pistas y que justifiquen el cobro por ir a las playas con tarifas tan altas, con ese criterio entonces todos los peruanos estariamos obligados a pagar 11 soles para poder bañarnos en cualquie playa de nuestro litoral, teniendo en cuenta que todos contribuimos con el país pagando nuestros impuestos directamente con los impuestos a nuestros sueldos y con los impuestos nuestros patrimonios privados que pagamos y indirectamente con los impuestos que pagamos al comprar los articulos consumidos diariamente, considerardo que nuestros impuestos son uno de los mas caros de la región, frente a esta problematica podriamos hacernos la siguiente pregunta, merecemos los peruanos pagar para el ingreso a una playa de nuestro mar Peruano y sobre todo si algunos hemos nacido en Ica?

text_clean: Estuve 10 años atrás en en parque de las aguas. Fue esoectacular. Pero ahora todo cambio muchísimo. Nada que ver con anterior. Sacaron 3/4 de todos los fuentes , no hay mas fuente de la pava con tasa. El parque de las aguas convertio en el parque de diverciones para los niños con atracciones . Entiendo, que hay que ganar plata de distintos medos, pero se fue magia del parque . 10 años atrás fue shoy excelente ( tengo videos), escuchamos música Vivaldi y otros compositores clásicos. La música sonaba constantemente sin parar. Ahora no, solamente con shoy , tampoco entendemos de que se trataba shoy . De reconquista? No me gustó, y no vuelvo más . Otra ves repito: si hacen dinero, quiero que los hacen bien. Si cobran los baños, hay que hacer los que la gente no esperan medio hora , que los limpian y ponen asiento. Los hinodoros de los baños son sucios y no tienen asientos, pero te cobran

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: As a native English speaker, I greatly appreciated English on some of the exhibits. You cannot expect English in all of them, and you can't bring your phone in, so be prepared to read some Spanish info without access to Google Translate. Even if you speak no Spanish at all, this museum is a must-see if you ever find yourself with some spare time in the Chiclayo area. They have some of the most spectacular jewlery that I have ever seen in a museum in Peru, the kind of stuff that ancient royalty used to wear, kept in perfect condition

text_clean: Don’t do the Nazca Line flight, it’s expensive and not that impressive. The tour costed $79 per person and when we get to the airport we still have to pay another 77 soles(about $21)for airport tax. The flight itself is horrible especially if you have motion sickness because the plane is small and very shaky. The pilot will just tell you to look left and right to look at the lines. Make you even more dizzy. The Nazca Line, in my opinion, doesn’t look like something ancient.

text_clean: Machu Picchu is a tourist trap, I don’t recommend going there. You pay for entrance ticket, train, bus, toilets, everything. And have to wait in lines in every point for so long. The trip from Cusco and back took 16 hours and more than 200 dollars for what??? Only 1 hour walk through the route. The ruins aren’t impressive because they are not original but rebuilt. Go to other ruins around Cusco instead. And the last recommendation: do not be caught on trap, treat locals as they treat you. Don’t overpay, bargain when possible, don’t leave tips because you were already changed twice, bring your own food and drinks to have a lunch.

text_clean: El sitio es increíble, le doy un 2 por la penosa organización que hay y el dinero que te dejas para poder verlo, un completo sin sentido. El primer punto negativo es tener que elegir que parte del lugar ver me parece un sinsentido, nosotros pagábamos por el tour 3A con Huaynapichu que no es especialmente económico y nos fuimos con la sensación de que nos faltaba por ver una gran parte. Además de que tienes el tiempo cobrado, en nuestro caso solo podíamos estar dentro 2h. Hay que añadir que antes de poder entrar hay que esperar una hora en el pueblo para que te suba el autobús, y después otros cuarenta minutos para entrar. Nuestra entrada era a las 7.00, terminamos entrando a las 7.30. El tour y la visita a la montaña fue bien, pero al salir estuvimos otra hora esperando hasta montar en el autobús de vuelta al pueblo. No me quiero imaginar estas esperas con frío o lluvia, pues estás completamente a la intemperie. Es algo que la autoridad competente tiene que pensar, no pueden tener a la gente en estas condiciones. En la vida habíamos visto algo así.

Nuestra entrada era a las 7.00, terminamos entrando a las 7.30. El tour y la visita a la montaña fue bien, pero al salir estuvimos otra hora esperando hasta montar en el autobús de vuelta al pueblo. No me quiero imaginar estas esperas con frío o lluvia, pues estás completamente a la intemperie. Es algo que la autoridad competente tiene que pensar, no pueden tener a la gente en estas condiciones. En la vida habíamos visto algo así.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The Royal Tombs of Sipán Museum near Chiclayo is a must-visit if you love history and archaeology. The pyramid-style architecture itself reflects the ancient Moche culture, and inside you walk through the story of the Lord of Sipán step by step, from excavation to the stunning treasures found in his tomb. The gold, silver, ceramics, and textiles give a deep insight into the artistry and beliefs of the Mochica civilization. The museum is very well maintained — clean, organized, and carefully curated with proper lighting and security that protect these priceless artifacts. The exhibits are presented with respect and detail, making it easy to appreciate the importance of this culture in Peru’s history. Some explanations are only in Spanish and weekends can be a bit crowded, but overall it is one of the best museums in Peru and highly recommended 😊😊😊

text_clean: I recently visited Machu Picchu with my partner, and while the site itself is undeniably breathtaking, I am deeply concerned about the lack of proper emergency response systems in place for visitors experiencing medical emergencies. During our visit, my partner, as he got out of the bus, slipped because of the uneven surface and slippery as the road was wet, he injured his ankle. He suffered a concerning medical episode— things unfolded very quickly - he was in a lot of pain, he collapsed and experienced convulsions - due to what we believe was a combination of altitude, and intense pain of the injury. In such a high-traffic and physically demanding site, we expected a prompt and trained medical response. Unfortunately, that was not the case. There was no visible or accessible medical staff nearby, and it took far too long to get any form of assistance. The staff present seemed untrained to handle emergencies such as this and there was no coordinated protocol to help us manage the situation. Thankfully my partner recovered but that was a very stressful moment for us. I am writing this not in anger, but in hopes that the management will: Review and strengthen their emergency protocols. Ensure that trained medical staff are stationed on-site in different posts at all times Install clearly marked emergency points or communication stations Train staff in basic first aid and response procedures Machu Picchu is one of the most visited tourist destinations in the world. With the number of visitors—including elderly people, children, and those not acclimated to the altitude—accidents and health emergencies are not rare, and a more robust system must be put in place to prevent avoidable harm. I truly hope the management takes this seriously. The experience itself is magical, but basic safety and human response must be part of that experience.

text_clean: Para ir primero, toman carro en la terminal hacia kuelap que está 7 soles. Luego toman el teleferico que está 20,40 soles por persona que incluye ida y vuelta en la couster y teleférico 🚡 y que solo lo pueden usar hasta la hora que te indican, sino deberás volver a pagar los 20,40 soles para regresar. Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.

Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.

text_clean: Las telecabinas son de última generación, es un paseo bastante interesante. Pero la fortaleza está aún muy lejos de ser un punto turístico como Machu Picchu. Hay que caminar sobre tierra, rocas y lodo para poder llegar a la cima en donde verán la fortaleza desde afuera porque no se puede ingresar al complejo. La caminata dura aproximadamente 2 horas y es muy difícil para niños y adultos mayores, incluso personas que no tienen mucha resistencia física.

text_clean: Para ir al machu picchu tienes que comprar las entradas con 2 o 3 meses de antelacion. He ido con mi familia y no es lo que esperaba, tienes que pagar por todo, si vas a subir a las montañas tienes que ir preparado con mucha agua y ropa comoda. Además para entrar al machu picchu tienes que pagar por circuitos, antes no hacia falta ya que con el circuito 1 se podia ver todo, ahora tienes que pagar por ver cada cosa, por ejemplo: en el circuito 1 solo ves el machu picchu desde fuera y subir la montaña, nada más. Si subes las montañas es bajo tu responsabilidad y si te caes o te haces daño tienes que pagar mucho dinero para un medico (creo que mas de 100 dolares). Tampoco es que al subir la montaña haiga una recompensa tremenda, subi la montaña del circuito 1 y solo habian unas sillas y un letrero de cuanta altura tiene la montaña. Puedes sacar buenas fotos pero nada más, si no traes agua, mejor ni lo intentes, tienes tiempo limite hasta las 12:00 mediodia para subir y se tarda unas 4 horas a paso lento. Si vas a subir tambien recomiendo llevar repelentes para los mosquitos ya que hay muchos mosquitos. Si te gusta escalar este viaje es para ti, pero si eres un poco sedentario no vale la pena pagar tanto solo para subir una montaña y tomarte unas cuantas fotos, por cierto, si llueve olvidate de subir los cerros, es imposible y probablemente te rompas o te hagas daño o peor. No hay seguridad alguna. Sinceramente es bonito para pasar el dia pero tienes que comprar los demas circuitos (1,2,3,4) , porque si no, no puedes ver nada. Recalco otra vez, tienes que pagar por todo, TODO. Las fotos son preciosas pero no todo lo que brilla es oro. Se podria distribuir mejor y que todo no sea tan caro. Igualmente muy bonito Perú y hermoso el machu picchu aunque con lo que cobran se podria mejor muchisimo.

text_clean: Hoy 01/feb/2026 totalmente descepcionada del servicio en este parque mágico del agua. Antes yo venía con mis hijos y podíamos sentarnos a relajarnos en el gras. Habían pocos juegos pero era divertido ver el espectaculo del agua y descansar en familia. Hoy es todo comercial al parecer han vendido las áreas del parque porque NO PERMITEN NI UN SEGUNDO SENTARSE EN EL GRAS. Ni a niños ni a nadie. Y lo peor es que NO HAY BANCAS SUFICIENTES PARA TODOS por lo que muchas personas niños, ancianos y adultos tenemos que estar parados o caminando o sentados en muritos muy incómodos. Esto se ha vuelto un negocio ya no es parque ya no es un área verde para compartir. Es un área lucrativa donde la Municipalidad está ganando y los ciudadanos no nos estamos beneficiando en nada. No vale la pena pagar para no tener por lo menos el beneficio de disfrutar de las áreas verdes. Si tienes hijos ven con minino 100 soles para los juegos comida aparte y si no tienes hijos hay lugares más bonitos en dónde pasear en pareja. No lo vale en serio no gasten ni su tiempo ni su dinero.

Hoy es todo comercial al parecer han vendido las áreas del parque porque NO PERMITEN NI UN SEGUNDO SENTARSE EN EL GRAS. Ni a niños ni a nadie. Y lo peor es que NO HAY BANCAS SUFICIENTES PARA TODOS por lo que muchas personas niños, ancianos y adultos tenemos que estar parados o caminando o sentados en muritos muy incómodos. Esto se ha vuelto un negocio ya no es parque ya no es un área verde para compartir. Es un área lucrativa donde la Municipalidad está ganando y los ciudadanos no nos estamos beneficiando en nada. No vale la pena pagar para no tener por lo menos el beneficio de disfrutar de las áreas verdes. Si tienes hijos ven con minino 100 soles para los juegos comida aparte y si no tienes hijos hay lugares más bonitos en dónde pasear en pareja. No lo vale en serio no gasten ni su tiempo ni su dinero.

I was hoping the museum (Maria Reiche) would give us mire insight, but that is more about her life and less about the lines...

Se nota que devastaron el lugar, pues la ciudad lo fue arruinando. Fue algo decepcionante ver obreros refaccionando la pirámide cual antiguos creadores de la misma

text_clean: Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Lo que menos nos gustó de nuestro viaje a Perú. Las líneas en sí son interesantes, pero todo lo que rodea a la gestión deja mucho que desear. Es muy caro para lo que ves, más el pago de 2 tasas diferentes una vez allí. Además, el vuelo en la avioneta dura media hora y las explicaciones son prácticamente inexistentes más allá de "aquí tenemos la figura del mono" En esa línea. Si pillara más de paso... Bueno. Pero pilla muy a desmano para solo media hora de sobrevuelo y ninguna explicación. Nos explicaron mil veces más cosas en Paracas sobre la única figura del candelabro que se encuentra allí, que en todas estas figuras en Nazca.

text_clean: Como siempre Macchu Picchu es el mejor lugar para recargar baterías y llenarse de energías. Por la Pandemia hay pocos turistas y se puede caminar tranquilamente y tomar muchas fotos con calma. Se cumplen todas las medidas de bioseguridad, es obligatorio el uso de mascarilla y protector facial en el bus y durante la caminata se debe tener la mascarilla en todo momento. Recomiendo pasar una noche en Aguas Calientes, ahora los hoteles tienen muchas ofertas, yo me hospedé en el Inti Punku Macchu Picchu Suites y el servicio fue estupendo, muy limpio, con habitaciones amplias, algunas con vista a la montaña e incluye el desayuno buffet. Si se quedan una noche después de ir a MP pueden hacer caminata a los Jardines de Mandor ..es un parque botánico silvestre que de encuentra a 1 hora más o menos ( depende del estado físico) el final del recorrido tiene una cascada increíble ( entrada 10 soles solo efectivo) . En el hotel les indican como llegar. Y para almorzar el restaurante Full House con vista al río, la comida es deliciosa y la atención de Cristian fue insuperable. Una experiencia maravillosa!!!

Si se quedan una noche después de ir a MP pueden hacer caminata a los Jardines de Mandor ..es un parque botánico silvestre que de encuentra a 1 hora más o menos ( depende del estado físico) el final del recorrido tiene una cascada increíble ( entrada 10 soles solo efectivo) . En el hotel les indican como llegar. Y para almorzar el restaurante Full House con vista al río, la comida es deliciosa y la atención de Cristian fue insuperable. Una experiencia maravillosa!!!

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo está cerrado y los restos reales de la tumba de Sipán no se pueden visitar. Según el personal, hay problemas con el aire acondicionado y tardarán un año en volver a abrir completamente al público. Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.

text_clean: El lugar y su historia así como también todo lo que han rescatado y recuperado es impresionante. Es una pena que tengan animales en cautiverio por el solo hecho de representar lo que hacían las comunidades indígenas en su entonces. Los cobayos se lastiman entre sí, las alpacas viven en espacios tan reducidos que no tienen pasto natural ni sombra de un árbol solo un techo de metal. Es lamentable que esto ocurra en el siglo XXI, pudieron recrearlo con maquetas o filmaciones sin necesidad de tener los animales en ese estado. Fue muy decepcionante.

text_clean: Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. En particular, los cuys están hacinados en grandes cantidades en espacios pequeños. Están sobre sus propias heces. En el poco tiempo que estuve ahí vi como uno atacaba a otros como resultado de estar tan apretados. No veo la necesidad de tener a estos animalitos en ese espacio tan reducido.

There are literally dozens of sellers trying to sell you all sorts of things, from coca leaves to umbrellas on a sunny day, specially if you are a tourist. Besides, now (Feb, 2018) Peru is going through a moment of political instability, so it's very likely to witness a bunch of ongoing protests at any giving time you visit the square. I actually like to watch those social movements, but that's a personal matter. Some may get a little bit scared by the powerful presence of police calvary.

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: La Reserva Natural es hermosa. Con acantilados que caen al mar, un paisaje agreste y desértico con el contraste del agua turquesa de fondo y mucha fauna. Tiene playas reparadas con aguas mansas y del lado opuesto aguas agitadas y ventosas. Se puede visitar toda la reserva en un dia.

label: positivo

text_clean: Al atardecer, llegando la noche, las luces empiezan a dar color y movimiento a las distintas piletas de agua, entre clásicas, túneles o chorros de diferente altura. El público toma ubicación en las distintas sillas ubicadas frente a la pileta central y empieza el espectáculo de luces y colores, entonces el parque se convierte en perfecta sincronía con la noche. Puedes ingresar después de adquirir tu entrada, visitantes nacionales o niños pagan 4 soles. Llegar antes de las 6pm te permite apreciar las distintas áreas verdes y monumentos. Interesante propuesta en Lima, para escapar de la agitación citadina.

text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.

text_clean: Tenía dudas de si aprovechar mi estancia en Lima para acudir al Circuito Mágico del Agua. Finalmente fui desde el centro andando y está bien para pasar la tarde. La entrada 🎟️ cuesta 5 soles y tienes jardines para descansar, puestos de comida y fuentes de agua que al caer la tarde se iluminan y confieren un ambiente único. También se hacen proyecciones sobre el agua de documentales que muestran la historia de Perú. Como malo destacar el altercado que se produjo con gente que accedió saltando las vallas para vender comida callejera y el personal del parque (a pesar de ser numeroso) no se pudo hacer con la situación. Se advierte de que no se compre comida a este tipo de personas (e incluso por megafonía) pero lo cierto es que había un montón de personas vendiendo lo que demuestra que los controles no son buenos… 😳

text_clean: Machu Picchu itself is amazing, but the way Peru deals with the tourism business leaves much to be desired. For starters, the Águas Calientes village (or Machu Picchu Pueblo) is nothing but a big tourist trap. It's dirty, the prices are extremely abusive and they treat tourists as cows that should be milked until the last drop. They have designed the ticket buying experience specifically to force people to stay there and be exploited for at least a couple of nights. You know, honesty is very important in tourism. People hate to feel like they're being abused. The Águas Calientes, and the Machu Picchu experience, for that matter, is far from honest. Although the Machu Picchu ruins are obviously really nice, I didn't like the whole experience at all. Overall, Peru is way behind their neighbors Ecuador, Chile and Argentina in the tourism game. It should try to learn something from them, especially how to treat tourists with respect and not as expendable money cows.

text_clean: Not to be missed of you're traveling in Arequipa and even better to make to the trip from Arequipa to Puno (or the other way around) much more interesting. Amazing landscapes going from tropical desert to alti plano, the agricultural terrasses in the value and the deepest canyon in the world. The absolute highlight was the condor viewing point where these beautiful animal fly few meters above your head. We took a two day tour Arequipa - Colca - Puno. Costed 87usd pp.

text_clean: I wished I had read the review before going to this site. Since I am in Lima with local, I did not bother researching, which resulted in rather disappointing experience. When you walk in, you have to wait for a guide. You cannot roam alone. We were told the next tour will start in 20 min later when we entered the site. We waited close to 30 min when the tour started. I asked if there is an English tour, and the response was 20 min later. I wish there was a set time so we did not have to sit and wait doing nothing. I decided to take the Spanish tour with my limited Spanish. I read this place up in Wiki and a few other websites night before. It is a cool place and had a great view on the top. I could not understand the majority of things guide said, and I was roaming alone among the groups. When the tour was done, there were about 5 guides sitting around there and doing nothing. I was thinking where were they before? Maybe night tour is much more popular, and all of them were gearing towards the night tour. Overall, I was disappointed at the experience.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: Amazing place, we went there at early hours so it wasn't very crowded. Although early its foggy so beware, sometimes it takes about an hour for it to clear out. We spent about 4hrs walking around checking the whole place out, walking at steady pace and stopping at every spot you're allowed and taking photos. Because of pandemic there are guards that will tell you to "catch up with your group" if they see you staying behind. They're just doing their jobs trying to keep us safe. 🤗 There is plenty of wildlife around, big and small so keep an eye open for those too if you are interested. If you are lucky you can spot Condors sometimes flying over, but difficult to capture with phone cameras because of the sun 😔

I consider that cruel and that I why i cannot comment except to say your handicapped policies suck!!!

text_clean: Pucllana Site Museum in Miraflores offers a fascinating glimpse into Lima’s pre-Incan history. The guided tours, included in the affordable entry fee, take you through the adobe pyramid, small museum exhibits, and a native flora and fauna area in about an hour. Its central location makes it easy to visit, and the contrast of ancient ruins amid the modern city is striking. While the site is historically significant, it’s relatively small and not as visually impressive as other Peruvian ruins. A worthwhile and educational stop if you’re nearby, but not a must-see if you’re short on time.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: We weren't sure if it would fit in our schedule for Peru at first, but I'm very glad we made a detour and planned some extra time for this. Absolutely stunning views and very varied flora and fauna. A few notes: the local authority Autocolca doesn't do their bookkeeping very well and I don't recommend getting E-tickets. Get your tickets in the early morning from their office in Chivay (plaza des armas) as you have the best chance of spotting the mighty condor in the morning, and they don't do much work there in the afternoon. Also I do not recommend going over the S109 (south south west) unless you have plenty of time (travel time with google maps is spot-on though) a sturdy car and good driving skills, because the road is undergoing about 100kms of heavy construction with poor driving conditions.

text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

text_clean: Pasé una muy mala experiencia en la zona de ingreso a la maravilla. Soy una mujer indígena kichwa Otavaleña (Ecuador) y llegué usando mi ropa tradicional (nada extravagante ni fuera de lugar). La srta de ingreso me impedía entrar indicando que no podía ingresar vestida así, a pesar de indicarle lo anterior. Se portó antipática, nada amable. Me dijo que use una chompa para cubrir mi ropa (no llevé chompa ni nada más). También dijo algo que yo iba a hacer videos de publicidad y cosas parecidas... le dije que no era así y que si deseaba podía revisar mi celular tanto al ingreso como a la salida. Simplemente no quisieron escuchar. Luego me mandaron a retirarme de la entrada con un guardia (Sr Guaman). Al cabo de nada, empecé a sufrir una crisis acompañada de taquicardia, llamaron a primeros auxilios, quienes me acompañaron hasta estabilizarme y luego me dejaron entrar. Fue una experiencia terrible. Necesitan más capacitación y empatia, les recuerdo que el mundo está lleno de diversidad cultural, que antes éramos el Tahuantinsuyo y que compartíamos y seguimos compartiendo creencias, tradiciones, lenguas y mucho más!

text_clean: Centro historico and Plaza de Armas (Plaza Mayor) are a mandatory stop for every tourist. Being that said, it's a little disappointing. Mainly because of the insecurity you feel. I've been in other South American historical centers (e.g., Quito) and it's different. As you reach the Plaza de Armas you're told to not step one street out of the Plaza or the two main roads. The plaza itself is quite large, with Lima's Cathedral on one side and several historical buildings nearby (most of them, being remodeled). It's nice and quite crowded with tourists and people trying to sell you things.

As you reach the Plaza de Armas you're told to not step one street out of the Plaza or the two main roads. The plaza itself is quite large, with Lima's Cathedral on one side and several historical buildings nearby (most of them, being remodeled). It's nice and quite crowded with tourists and people trying to sell you things.
label: neutro

text_clean: De reserva solo tiene el nombre y lo que te cobran en la entrada. Apenas hay carteles informativos sobre las especies y su estado de conservación, época de cría o paso por la reserva. Hay pescadores pescando con red, varias personas retirando las algas de la playa ( no sé si es por turismo o pesca) que son ecosistemas super importantes para mucha fauna que vive en ellas. Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona

text_clean: I came to Arequipa to enjoy the city and to visit the Monastery was something I was excited about doing. I arrived yesterday and I was extremely disappointed to find that there was no way for someone like me to enter and enjoy this beautiful piece of architecture and history.. I have muscular dystrophy and to use stairs is basically impossible for me. I understand that it is a historic city and things sometimes can’t be changed for preservation purposes - but to have no ramp or mini platform elevator or alternate entrance or even a handrail for the stairs was shocking and disappointing to say the least. I only write this review to let others know who may be physically altered (senior citizens or pregnant women) or handicapped like me that this will be what you find when you arrive there. It makes no sense for a place that is so historical and beautiful and important, a tourist treasure of the city, to not be accesible to everyone. I was still able to enjoy some of the beautiful architecture from the outside, but it was very sad not to be able to experience this.

text_clean: Entrada súper económica, pero luego entras y el espectáculo es pobrisimo, no han invertido ni medio, imágenes y sonido de pésima calidad, todo el parque está tomado por juegos pagos en muy mal estado (comenzando por la limpieza), las luces de los mismos interfieren con el “espectáculo”. Cuando quieres ir a ver las piletas te quieren hacer otro cobro adicional porque ahora lo han puesto “instagrameable” Los antiguos puestos de comida y postres tradicionales han sido reemplazados por locales de algodón radioactivo y pollo frito. No lo recomiendo.

text_clean: Disappointing :-( First of all, my experience has nothing to do with the actual site. The view, ruins, landscape and llamas are surreal! My disappointing experience is about the management of the site. I knew it was a touristy place and quite expensive to go. Most places are restricted/controlled and you can't enter inside the temples etc..you can just go around. Apparently a few years ago everything was open (said some friends who went before). And then you have all the tour groups that take a lot of spaces on the path. I almost didn't take any pictures, I didn't feel I had the freedom to do so because the path goes only on one way (so you can't go back on your path) and people are moving so you kind of feel a bit in a tour group without being in it and you kind of have to go forward. I didn't feel free to walk around/take pictures as I would have liked to. And then there are 4 choices of tickets, with different circuits, bit it doesn't give you access to everywhere with only one ticket, so you need to buy another one. This wasn't very cleared on the website. I just find that you put so much effort (physically, mentally and financially) to get there (waking up super early, doing all the trip to get to Aguas Calientes, walking up the (many!) stairs to the entrance, and the cost etc), in the end to leave with a bitter feeling. Is it worth it? I'm still unsure if it was.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

They wear reflective vests and kinda look official but they aren't. Park your car inside the complex.

text_clean: It was an amazing experience and although it was very busy it wasn't crowded as you can see in the pictures. Fyi, the pictures do not do the site justice. Its fantastic!! You can walk up if you feel you need a challenge or you can go up in a bus from the town of Machu Picchu. The queue looks horrendous but it moves quickly and takes about half an hour or so. Up at the top there is loo and you can store luggage so you dont have to drag it up. The start is pretty strenuous and steep with about 200 hundred steps straight up (which is more difficult if you've not yet fully acclimatised to the altitude). From then on it's all a bit easier and the views leave you breathless (as well as the altitude). I'd say that you need to bring lots of water with you because you dehydrate quickly in the heat and strong sun (husband and I brought two litres which wasn't enough). You also definitely need sunblock unless you don't mind burning to a crisp. We saw no evidence of mosquitos! It is also pretty normal for it to rain and thunder so you'll need to come prepared for that. You can visit without a guide but I think you'll not get to appreciate the site as much. At the end you can also walk back down to the town or again just take the bus. Again the queue looks bad but moves very quick.

text_clean: I really hate 1 star reviews for this historical site but I am writing one. Due to the protests in September of 2025 we were unable to get our train to Aguas Caliente. I completely support people's right to protest and was unwilling to travel to MP during this dispute. Some people travelled by van for 5 hours and walked for several more miles to access the town. I chose to stay in Ollantaytambo for a couple of extra days which was really enjoyable. I am giving MP 1 star because despite the issues people have accessing MP their refund process is impossible. I was told I need to submit personal information including pictures of my passport. They sent me a link to do this. The link does not say anything about the purpose of why I am submitting this information. I am wary of providing this information without a guarantee of getting a refund. The ticketing website has no information about asking for refunds. Surely they are aware that thousands of people were unable to go to MP due to the strike. Perurail actually sent an email explaining the refund process. I imagine the ministry of tourism made quite a bit of money from no-show tickets.

text_clean: Esta junto a la playa " El Raspon" y las caracteristicas son similares, esta ubicada en el Area de conservacion protegida de la peninsula de Paracas ( Dpto. De Ica ). La via de acceso tiene pesima señalizacion y es insegura. Es una limpia playa de arena y agua agradable, el nombre de la playa se debe a la existencia del yacimiento de carbon del cual se servian los lugareños en la antiguedad, hay que decender aprox. 35 mts. Por escalinatas de concreto bien construidas para llegar a la playa , se puede observar fauna marina y aves guaneras, existen formaciones geologicas y cavidades producidas por la erosion marina y vientos , en estas se observan tambien restos fosiles de flora y fauna asi como capas de diferentes etapas de formacion geologica. Por el tema del decenso a la playa; no recomiendo estas playas para las personas con discapacidad en las piernas o de edad muy avanzada. Hay estacionamiento y seguridad

text_clean: Climbed this. Huge Inca stairs, not an easy 2 hour climb. One buster on the way down. Not sure it's worth it. Use the time to go to the Inca bridge. If you do want to go, you have to buy tickets in advance the day before. There are daily quotas. The only reason people go up there is because they are already invested because they bought the ticket. Machu Picchu is tiny from up there. The views from the top - I've seen better.

text_clean: The fountains are very nice and it could be a relaxing and enjoyable park. The park staff and police are trying to get a handle on the informal(illegal) venders in the park. Which for the most part are not that big of a deal, but when you are watching the fountain and light show and there is some guy repeatedly yelling out whatever he is selling and throwing things around to'advertise' it gets old real quick. All the police and park staff do is stand there with them. They don't actually remove them.
